pick.rationale: This Sweet 16 matchup features two top-7 teams with a compelling strength-on-strength battle: Illinois's nation-leading offense (#1 in KenPom at 131.2 adjusted offensive efficiency) against Houston's elite defense (#4 nationally at 91.85). While Illinois's offense is elite, Houston's suffocating defense has consistently neutralized high-powered attacks in Big 12 play, and the Cougars' 5th-ranked overall efficiency includes a strong offense (13th-16th) that can exploit Illinois's 24th-ranked defense. Houston's tournament pedigree under Kelvin Sampson—five straight Sweet 16 appearances and last year's national championship game run—provides a significant edge in March, and guards Milos Uzan and Emanuel Sharp have played through their injury concerns to lead Houston to the Big 12 championship game. Illinois freshman Keaton Wagler's emergence (26 points vs. Penn) gives the Illini upset potential, but Houston's defensive prowess and proven ability to win close tournament games gives them a narrow advantage in this evenly-matched showdown.

result.text: Is this the year for Kelvin Sampson, Houston in NCAA March Madness? Skip to main content Home Sports on TV High School Sooners OKC Thunder Cowboys Big 12 SEC Sports Outdoors SportsbookWire MMA Junkie Golf Week Is this the year for Kelvin Sampson, Houston to win March Madness? Joe Mussatto The Oklahoman Maybe this will be the year for Kelvin Sampson . After coming oh so close a season ago , falling to Florida in the national championship game, maybe Houston can go all the way. In this week’s March Madness edition of Mussatto’s Minutes, we break down the first-round matchups for the Oklahoma and Oklahoma State women, give you reasons to root for each of the eight tournament teams in OKC and highlight a local school making a run in the Division II Tournament. But we start with Sampson and the No. 2 seed Cougars, who will open the tournament here in Oklahoma City against No. 15 seed Idaho at 9:10 p.m. Thursday. Houston has been one of the best programs in men’s college basketball since the turn of the decade. The Cougars have either been a No. 1 or No. 2 seed in five of the last six NCAA Tournaments. Houston has advanced to the Sweet 16 or further in each of the last five seasons. More: March Madness upset picks, surprise predictions for 2026 men's NCAA basketball bracket 2020-21: Lost to eventual-champion Baylor in Final Four 2021-22: Lost to Villanova in Elite Eight 2022-23: Lost to Miami in Sweet 16 2023-24: Lost to Duke in Sweet 16 2024-25: Upset Cooper Flagg and Duke in national semifinal, lost to Florida in final This isn’t Sampson’s best Houston team, but these Cougars (28-6) could absolutely be the ones to break through. Houston trounced Kansas in the Big 12 Tournament before a close loss to Arizona (my national title pick) in the championship game. Freshman guard Kingston Flemings will be a top-10 pick, maybe top-five, in the upcoming NBA draft. Senior guards Milos Uzan and Emanuel Sharp and junior forward Joseph Tugler have been around forever. Freshman center Chris Cenac Jr. is another projected first-rounder. And Sampson, of course, is one of the game’s best coaches. The former OU coach of 12 seasons is now in Year 12 at Houston. He’s flanked on the bench by his son, former Sooner guard Kellen Sampson, and an all-time Sooner in Hollis Price. Kelvin Sampson is showing zero signs of slowing down, but at 70, he’d become the oldest coach to ever win a national championship. UConn’s Jim Calhoun was 68 when he led the Huskies to the 2011 title. Florida’s Todd Golden, at 39, became the ninth-youngest coach to win a title when his Gators beat Sampson’s Cougars last March. Among active coaches, only Tom Izzo (59), Bill Self (53), John Calipari (50), Mark Few (44), Rick Pitino (40) and Rick Barnes (33) have more NCAA Tournament wins than Sampson (31). Few, Barnes and Sampson are still looking for their first championship. Maybe this will be the year for Sampson. result.text: Is this the year for Kelvin Sampson, Houston in NCAA March Madness? Skip to main content Home Sports on TV High School Sooners OKC Thunder Cowboys Big 12 SEC Sports Outdoors SportsbookWire MMA Junkie Golf Week Is this the year for Kelvin Sampson, Houston to win March Madness? Joe Mussatto The Oklahoman Maybe this will be the year for Kelvin Sampson . After coming oh so close a season ago , falling to Florida in the national championship game, maybe Houston can go all the way. In this week’s March Madness edition of Mussatto’s Minutes, we break down the first-round matchups for the Oklahoma and Oklahoma State women, give you reasons to root for each of the eight tournament teams in OKC and highlight a local school making a run in the Division II Tournament. But we start with Sampson and the No. 2 seed Cougars, who will open the tournament here in Oklahoma City against No. 15 seed Idaho at 9:10 p.m. Thursday. Houston has been one of the best programs in men’s college basketball since the turn of the decade. The Cougars have either been a No. 1 or No. 2 seed in five of the last six NCAA Tournaments. Houston has advanced to the Sweet 16 or further in each of the last five seasons. More: March Madness upset picks, surprise predictions for 2026 men's NCAA basketball bracket 2020-21: Lost to eventual-champion Baylor in Final Four 2021-22: Lost to Villanova in Elite Eight 2022-23: Lost to Miami in Sweet 16 2023-24: Lost to Duke in Sweet 16 2024-25: Upset Cooper Flagg and Duke in national semifinal, lost to Florida in final This isn’t Sampson’s best Houston team, but these Cougars (28-6) could absolutely be the ones to break through. Houston trounced Kansas in the Big 12 Tournament before a close loss to Arizona (my national title pick) in the championship game. Freshman guard Kingston Flemings will be a top-10 pick, maybe top-five, in the upcoming NBA draft. Senior guards Milos Uzan and Emanuel Sharp and junior forward Joseph Tugler have been around forever. Freshman center Chris Cenac Jr. is another projected first-rounder. And Sampson, of course, is one of the game’s best coaches. The former OU coach of 12 seasons is now in Year 12 at Houston. He’s flanked on the bench by his son, former Sooner guard Kellen Sampson, and an all-time Sooner in Hollis Price. Kelvin Sampson is showing zero signs of slowing down, but at 70, he’d become the oldest coach to ever win a national championship. UConn’s Jim Calhoun was 68 when he led the Huskies to the 2011 title. Florida’s Todd Golden, at 39, became the ninth-youngest coach to win a title when his Gators beat Sampson’s Cougars last March. Among active coaches, only Tom Izzo (59), Bill Self (53), John Calipari (50), Mark Few (44), Rick Pitino (40) and Rick Barnes (33) have more NCAA Tournament wins than Sampson (31). Few, Barnes and Sampson are still looking for their first championship. Maybe this will be the year for Sampson.
